rulings.Case Title: DANIEL PIERINI VS. COSMOPOLITAN PRIVATE SECURITY, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Daniel Pierini'S Notice Of Motion To Set Aside And Vacate Judgment (From 12-1)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, January 4, 2012, Line 1. Plaintiff Daniel Pierini'S Notice Of Motion To Set Aside And Vacate Judgment (From 12-1). The motion was continued from December 5, when the court granted plaintiff's request to set aside the judgment of dismissal and set January 4 for a hearing on defendants'' motion to dismiss for delay in prosecution. The motion to dismiss is denied due to the strong policy favoring deciding cases on their merits and the absence of any prejudice to defendants from the delay. rulings.Case Title: RIMMA BOROKHOV et al VS. FREEDOM WEST HOMES, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Freedom West Homes Incorporated Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defendants Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, January 4, 2012, line 2,(continuation of tentative ruling) Defendant Freedom West Homes Incorporated Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defendants Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions -- and persons identified as Does 10, 14, and 15 in Plaintiff Michael Tso's response to Specially Prepared Interrogatory No. 1. Nevertheless Nevertheless, Plaintiffs shall disclose their identities and contact information to Defendants' counsel in order to allow Defendants' counsel to depose them. Defendants' counsel may not reveal the information to the Defendants or Defendants' agents, and neither Defendants nor their agents may attend any such depositions of these persons. 3) Declarations of Individuals Identified as Does 2, 3, 4, 7, and 8. Plaintiffs shall provide Defendants' counsel with unredacted copies of the declarations in Exhibit G to the Declaration of Christopher B. Allard in Support of Defendants' Motion to Compel Further Discovery Responses in order to allow Defendants' counsel to depose the declarants. Counsel for Defendants may not share the unredacted copies of the declarations with the Defendants or their agents, and neither Defendants nor their agents may attend the depositions of these persons. Plaintiffs shall provide a second redacted set of the declarations to counsel for Defendants. The redacted set shall be identical to the declarations that appear as Exhibit G to the Declaration of Christopher B. Allard in Support of Defendants' Motion to Compel Further Discovery Responses, except that they shall disclose the names of the board members that were previously redacted from the declarations. Defendants' counsel may share the redacted set with Defendants and their agents. Defendants' and Plaintiffs' requests for monetary sanctions against the other party are denied. rulings.Case Title: RIMMA BOROKHOV et al VS. FREEDOM WEST HOMES, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defendants' Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, January 4, 2012, line 2 (CONTINUATION OF TENTATIVE RULING) Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defendants' Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions - In opposition to Defendants' motion, Plaintiffs assert that the witnesses have a right to privacy in their names and addresses, and would be subject to retaliation if their identities become known. Plaintiffs' complaint alleges a number of instances of harassment by Defendants including vandalism to their automobiles and the automobiles of family members, one incident allegedly resulting in a tie blowout on a freeway that was caused by a three-inch metal screw placed in Plaintiff Borokhov's tire (Complaint Paragraph 63). The names and contact information of the witnesses is presumptively discoverable under Code of Civil Procedure Section 2017.010, which states that "[d]iscovery may be obtained of the identity and location of persons having knowledge of any discoverable matter . . . ." (emphasis added). Plaintiffs concede as much in their opposition memorandum of points and authorities, admitting that "[t]he unredacted declarations along with information they contain are admittedly responsive to [Defendants'] requests" (Plaintiffs' Memorandum of Points and Authorities in Opposition to Defendants' Motion to Compel at 9:5-6). Plaintiffs nonetheless assert that disclosure of the identities and home addresses of the witnesses would violate their right to privacy under the California Constitution and subject them to retaliation. A violation of the constitutional right of privacy is only established where three conditions are shown: '(1) a legally protected privacy interest; (2) a reasonable expectation of privacy in the circumstances; and (3) conduct by defendant constituting a serious invasion of privacy.' Johnson v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th 1050, 1068 [95 Cal.Rptr.2d 864, 876]. Under certain circumstances the right to privacy can include the right to remain anonymous. See Johnson v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th 1050, 1070 (holding that anonymous sperm donor had right to privacy concerning his identity, but balancing that right against the public interest in the health of the children).

rulings.Case Title: RIMMA BOROKHOV et al VS. FREEDOM WEST HOMES, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Alton Management Corporation Renotice Of Hearing On Defendants Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, January 4, 2012, line 2,(CONTINUATION OF TENTATIVE), Defendant Alton Management Corporation Renotice Of Hearing On Defendants Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses - The constitutional right to privacy is not absolute and must therefore be balanced against other important interests. Johnson v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th 1050, 1070. Because discovery orders involve state-compelled disclosure, such disclosure is treated as a product of state action. Id. at 1071. The state has a compelling interest in making certain that parties comply with properly served subpoenas and discovery orders in order to disclose relevant information to the fullest extent allowable. Second, the state has an interest in seeking the truth in court proceedings. Id. at 1071. The trial court has a duty to reconcile the conflicting interests of litigants in obtaining necessary discovery and third parties in maintaining privacy. Schnabel v. Superior Court (1993) 5 Cal.4th 704, 712. The considerations which, among others, will affect the exercise of the trial court's discretion include: "the purpose of the information sought, the effect that disclosure will have on the parties and on the trial, the nature of the objections urged by the party resisting disclosure, and ability of the court to make an alternative order which may grant partial disclosure, disclosure in another form, or disclosure only in the event that the party seeking the information undertakes certain specified burdens which appear just under the circumstances.' Id. at 712. Where a right to privacy exists, "the trial court is required to limit the scope of inquiry to the extent necessary to a fair resolution of the case. Schnabel v. Superior Court (1993) 5 Cal.4th 704, 714. In evaluating privacy claims for the protection of third parties, a court should consider its "ability to make an alternative order which may grant partial disclosure, disclosure in another form, or disclosure only in the event that the party seeking the information undertakes certain specified burdens which appear just under the circumstances. Johnson v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th at 1073. Where is possible to do so, "... the courts should impose partial limitations rather than an outright denial of discovery. Id. In the Johnson v. Superior Court case, the Court of Appeal held that the party seeking discovery was entitled to take the anonymous sperm-donor's deposition to delve into his and his family's health and medical history, but that his identity was to be "protected to the fullest extent possible and the identities of his family members are not to be disclosed." Johnson v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th at 1073. The Court of Appeal, in remanding the case to the Superior Court, explicitly approved a procedure whereby the deposition of the anonymous witness could proceed, but attendance at the deposition could be limited to the parties' counsel and the deposition transcript might refer simply to "John Doe" as the deponent." Johnson v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th at 1072. With these principles in mind, the Court turns to the specific discovery requests that are the subject of Defendants' motion to compel.

rulings.Case Title: RIMMA BOROKHOV et al VS. FREEDOM WEST HOMES, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Alton Management Corporation, Paula Butler & William Hutton'S Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defendants Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, January 4, 2012, line 2, CONTINUATION OF TENTATIVE - Defendants Alton Management Corporation, Paula Butler & William Hutton'S Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defendants Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions - 1) Identity of Board Members and Agents of Defendant Alton Management Corporation Who Allegedly Solicited Bribes: Plaintiffs' rationale for redacting the names or physical descriptions of board members and their agents who solicited bribes (namely, to protect them from retaliation from the people who allegedly used their positions to solicit the bribes) does not apply where the people whose identity has been withheld are the ones who allegedly solicited the bribes. Therefore, whatever right to privacy these people have in their identity under the circumstances cannot outweigh the admittedly discoverable nature of the information. Plaintiffs shall provide Defendants with the identity of all people identified as [Board Member] or "[BOARD MEMBER"] in Plaintiffs' responses to Specially Prepared Interrogatory No. 1. Plaintiff Borokhov shall provide Defendants with the name of "[Doe 38]" identified in Plaintiff's response to Specially Prepared Interrogatory No. 1 as a person working in the administrative offices of the cooperative. Defendants' counsel may share this information with the Defendants and their agents. 2) Identity and Addresses of Does Alleged By Plaintiffs As Having Been Required To Pay Bribes To Obtain Their Apartments. Plaintiff Borokhov's response to Specially Prepared Interrogatory No. 1 states that during or around the late summer of 2008 a person came into a meeting attended by 20 to 30 cooperative members and screamed that the person "paid a board member $20,000 to get an apartment but was evicted." Plaintiff Borokhov has redacted the identifying information concerning the board member and the person who made the public allegation, identifying them as "[BOARD MEMBER]" and "PHYSICAL DESCRIPTION]." Neither of these persons can have a reasonable expectation of privacy in their identity after this public accusation made during the meeting. Plaintiff Borokhov shall provide Defendants' counsel with the information redacted from Plaintiff's response to Special Interrogatory No. 1 concerning the persons identified as "[BOARD MEMBER]" and "[PHYSICAL DESCRIPTION]." This information may be shared with the Defendants. By contrast, all three Plaintiffs' responses to Special Interrogatory No. 1 demonstrate that unidentified "Does" who the Plaintiffs contend were required to pay bribes in order to obtain their apartments did not disclose those solicitations publicly. These individuals have a right to privacy, under the circumstances, to have their identities protected in order to prevent the possibility of retaliation against them and to prevent any chilling effect that such disclosure could have on future complaints. See City of San Jose v. Superior Court (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 1008, 1023-24 (holding that, "airport noise complainants have a significant privacy interest in their names, addresses, and telephone numbers as well as in the fact that they have made a complaint to their government, and that disclosure of this information would have a chilling effect on future complaints"). The individuals include persons identified as Does 35 and 36 in Plaintiff Borokhov's response to Specially Prepared Interrogatory No. 1; persons identified as Does 3, 4, 5, 7, 9, 10, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 30, 31, and 32 in Plaintiff David Tse's response to Specially Prepared Interrogatory No. 1;

rulings.Case Title: RIMMA BOROKHOV et al VS. FREEDOM WEST HOMES, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defts' Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, January 4, 2012, line 2, DEFENDANT ALTON MANAGEMENT CORPORATION, PAULA BUTLER, WILLIAM HUTTON Re-Notice Of Hearing On Defts' Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Ernst Halperin, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Department 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' motion to compel further discovery sanctions and request for sanctions is GRANTED IN PART and DENIED IN PART. Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that, in a period of six months, Defendants brought four meritless and ultimately unsuccessful lawsuits in unlawful detainer against Plaintiffs in connection with a scheme to wrongfully evict residents of a cooperative apartment complex in order to profit by soliciting and accepting bribes from potential new residents seeking to become shareholders in the cooperative. Defendants seek to compel Plaintiffs to disclose information Plaintiffs have redacted from their responses to Defendants' special interrogatories. This information consists of the names and addresses of a number of third-party witnesses. Plaintiffs allege that these witnesses have either told Plaintiffs that they were solicited for and did pay bribes in order to become residents in the apartment development and shareholders in the cooperative that owns the apartments, or have witnessed the solicitation of bribes by one or more unidentified Board Members of the cooperative. Defendants also seek to compel Plaintiffs to produce unredacted copies of declarations from five of these witnesses.

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. = (302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: WUNDER BREWING COMPANY, INC. et al VS. ZACK KHOURY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR WITHDRAWAL OF ATTORNEY OF RECORD
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, January 4, 2012, Line 19. ATTORNEY'S MOTION FOR WITHDRAWAL AS ATTORNEY OF RECORD FOR PLAINTIFFS CARL DURHAM AND AUGUSTUS VASTARDIS. Granted to the extent that a bankruptcy court automatic stay is not in place as to either or both of the two parties that permission is sought to withdraw as counsel. If an automatic stay is in place, the court does not intend to make any order contrary to the stay. No opposition filed and good cause shown. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. = (302/HEK)
